title: Breaking Bottlenecks in Solid Electrolyte Discovery with Large Artificial Intelligence Models
authors: Eric Jianfeng Cheng, Min Hong, Zhiquan Zeng, Chuanyu Liu, Qian Wang, Melissa Jane Meadowcroft
abstract: Solid electrolytes (SEs) are central to next-generation metal batteries, yet their discovery remains constrained by fragmented data, limited transferability of simulations, and slow experimental iteration. Unlike catalysis, where surface reactivity dominates, SEs require simultaneous optimization of bulk ion transport, defect chemistry, mechanical integrity, and interfacial stability. Here, we outline a framework for autonomous SE discovery enabled by large artificial intelligence (AI) models, including machine learning interatomic potentials (MLIPs) and large language models (LLMs). We discuss the evolution from static materials databases to dynamic, self-updating knowledge systems, the role of MLIPs in bridging density functional theory (DFT) and long-timescale ion migration, and the emergence of LLMs as engines for literature mining, hypothesis generation, and scientific reasoning. We further describe a closed-loop architecture integrating AI-driven candidate design, multiscale simulation, uncertainty-aware selection, and experimental validation. Such systems shift SE research from intuition-guided exploration to data-informed, self-improving cycles. We conclude by highlighting challenges in data standardization, interfacial complexity, and reproducibility, and we propose design principles for building autonomous laboratories for solid-state battery materials.

infographic.key_insight: Large AI models can connect data mining, simulation, and experiment to accelerate solid electrolyte discovery in a closed-loop workflow.
infographic.engineering_relevance: This framework could speed the design of safer, longer-life solid-state batteries by improving how bulk transport, defects, mechanics, and interfaces are optimized together.
method: Use MLIPs to bridge DFT and long-timescale ion migration.
Use LLMs for literature mining, hypothesis generation, and scientific reasoning.
Combine AI candidate design, uncertainty-aware selection, simulation, and experimental validation in a closed loop.
infographic.evidence_signal: The abstract presents a framework and rationale for autonomous solid electrolyte discovery, but does not report specific benchmark results or quantitative performance data.
infographic.watch_item: A major implementation risk is poor data standardization and reproducibility, especially for complex interfacial phenomena.
infographic.paper_title: Breaking Bottlenecks in Solid Electrolyte Discovery with Large Artificial Intelligence Models

title: A Differentiable DFT-Based Framework for Inverse Materials Design
authors: Kohei Ishii, Hisazumi Akai, Tetsuya Fukushima, Hikari Shinya, Koji Inui
abstract: Discovering solid-state materials with target properties remains a central challenge in computational materials science. Existing approaches -- high-throughput screening, surrogate optimization, and generative models -- require extensive evaluations or training data and extrapolate poorly to unseen compositions. Here we develop a first-principles inverse-design framework, integrating reverse-mode automatic differentiation (AD) into KKR-CPA -- the Korringa--Kohn--Rostoker method with the coherent potential approximation -- where atomic compositions are continuous variables to be optimized. Reverse-mode AD yields gradients of objective functions with respect to composition at a cost independent of the number of candidate elements, enabling gradient-based optimization to identify materials from compositional spaces spanning dozens of elements. In this framework, any computable quantity can serve as the objective. We demonstrate this generality through two contrasting applications, magnetic alloys and half-metals, yielding candidates such as (Lu$_{0.553}$Yb$_{0.447}$)(Co$_{0.759}$Fe$_{0.241}$)$_2$Fe$_3$ and FeZr(Sb$_{0.94}$Te$_{0.06}$). Our framework offers a physically grounded route from a target property to the material that realizes it.

infographic.key_insight: The paper introduces a differentiable first-principles inverse-design framework that uses reverse-mode automatic differentiation inside KKR-CPA to optimize material composition directly for a target property.
infographic.engineering_relevance: This enables scalable, gradient-based search over large compositional spaces without needing training data, making inverse materials design more physically grounded and potentially more efficient.
method: Embed reverse-mode AD in KKR-CPA.
Treat atomic compositions as continuous optimization variables.
Use gradients of any computable objective to drive composition search.
infographic.evidence_signal: The authors demonstrate the framework on two example targets—magnetic alloys and half-metals—producing candidate compositions such as (Lu0.553Yb0.447)(Co0.759Fe0.241)2Fe3 and FeZr(Sb0.94Te0.06).
infographic.watch_item: The approach depends on the fidelity and numerical stability of the underlying KKR-CPA model, so predicted optima still need validation with higher-level calculations or experiments.
infographic.paper_title: A Differentiable DFT-Based Framework for Inverse Materials Design

title: Graph Neural Networks Applications Across Domains: All Insights You Need
authors: Abderaouf Bahi
abstract: Graph neural networks have moved from a niche representation-learning technique to the default model class wherever data carry relational structure. The interesting question is no longer whether message passing helps on a given dataset, but where graph structure earns its computational cost and where it does not. This survey organises the field around a single design space, derives the spectral and spatial formulations from shared first principles, and connects expressive power to the Weisfeiler-Leman hierarchy with explicit statements of what current architectures can and cannot separate. Against that methodological backbone we examine twelve application domains, among them recommendation and social networks, knowledge graphs and language-model integration, drug discovery and molecular property learning, healthcare and neuroscience, computer vision, traffic and urban computing, power and renewable-energy systems, wireless and sixth-generation networks, fraud and cybersecurity, industrial prognostics, materials science, and climate modelling. For each domain we specify the graph-construction choices and their costs, identify which architecture families dominate and why, and separate reported gains from artefacts of weak baselines or favourable splits. A cross-domain comparison exposes recurring patterns: heterophily and scale undercut the same models almost everywhere, temporal graphs remain harder than their static counterparts, and the architectures that top public leaderboards are seldom the ones that reach deployment. We treat over-smoothing, over-squashing, robustness, distribution shift, fairness, and explainability not as a closing checklist but as the constraints that decide adoption.

infographic.key_insight: Graph neural networks are now a general-purpose choice for relational data, but their real value depends on when graph structure truly justifies the added computation.
infographic.engineering_relevance: For materials discovery and other engineering domains, the paper frames GNN success around graph construction quality, expressive power, and deployment constraints rather than leaderboard scores alone.
method: Unifies spectral and spatial GNN views from shared first principles
Connects model expressiveness to the Weisfeiler-Leman hierarchy
Compares 12 application domains using architecture choice, graph construction cost, and baseline quality
infographic.evidence_signal: The survey synthesizes cross-domain results and explicitly separates reported gains from weak baselines or favorable data splits.
infographic.watch_item: Heterophily, temporal dynamics, and distribution shift often reduce GNN effectiveness, so strong offline results may not transfer cleanly to deployment.
infographic.paper_title: Graph Neural Networks Applications Across Domains: All Insights You Need

title: CoTAR: Topology and Atomic State Reconstruction in Condensed Phases
authors: Hodaka Mori, Yu Miyazaki, Takechika Kikkawa
abstract: Universal machine learning interatomic potentials (uMLIPs) enable condensed-phase molecular dynamics (MD) simulations with near-first-principles accuracy, but their lack of explicit molecular topology limits bond-aware analysis and reconnection to classical force fields. Here, we present CoTAR, a hybrid graph neural network (GNN)--hidden Markov model (HMM) framework that reconstructs molecular topology, formal charges, and unpaired electrons from atomic species, coordinates, and total charge by combining message passing on a proximity graph with a van der Waals prior, chemical constraints, and temporal smoothing. Across 128 nonreactive, topology-preserving condensed-phase systems, CoTAR achieved a bond-order-weighted F1 score of 0.906 on classical-MD data; for uMLIP trajectories, few-shot fine-tuning improved the valid-snapshot rate from 38.6\% to 84.7\%. The reconstructed topologies also supported downstream classical MD simulations, and HMM smoothing improved system-level MD simulation feasibility from 83.6\% to 85.9\%, indicating that CoTAR provides a practical framework for bond-aware analysis of condensed-phase uMLIP trajectories.

infographic.key_insight: CoTAR reconstructs molecular topology, formal charges, and unpaired electrons from condensed-phase trajectories by combining a GNN with HMM-based temporal smoothing and chemical priors.
infographic.engineering_relevance: It makes universal ML interatomic potentials easier to analyze and reconnect to classical force fields, enabling bond-aware downstream MD workflows from atomistic simulation data.
method: Builds a proximity graph from atomic species and coordinates and applies message passing with a van der Waals prior.
Uses chemical constraints plus a hidden Markov model to smooth topology predictions over time.
Infers bonds, formal charges, and unpaired electrons from total charge and local atomic environments.
infographic.evidence_signal: Across 128 topology-preserving condensed-phase systems, CoTAR reached a bond-order-weighted F1 score of 0.906 on classical-MD data, and few-shot fine-tuning raised valid-snapshot rate on uMLIP trajectories from 38.6% to 84.7%.
infographic.watch_item: Performance depends on topology-preserving, nonreactive systems, so robustness on strongly reactive or bond-breaking trajectories may be limited.
infographic.paper_title: CoTAR: Topology and Atomic State Reconstruction in Condensed Phases

title: Ductility Design Rules for Tungsten based Refractory High Entropy Alloys from Sparse Experimental Datasets
authors: Avik Mahata, Nick Beaver
abstract: Tungsten-based refractory high-entropy alloys (RHEAs) are promising materials for fusion applications but often remain brittle at room temperature because of tungsten's high ductile-to-brittle transition temperature (DBTT). To identify alloying strategies that improve ductility, we compiled a curated dataset of experimentally reported tungsten-containing alloys and developed composition-based, physics-informed descriptors for machine learning. Three classifiers were evaluated using nested cross-validation, and a support vector classifier (SVC) showed the best generalization for this sparse dataset. Shapley additive explanations identified the exchange-correlation parameter, valence electron concentration, pressure field, and electronegativity mismatch as the most influential features governing ductile-brittle behavior. Synthetic compositions generated within the convex interpolation domain of the training data were evaluated and visualized on pseudo-ternary diagrams to map predicted ductility trends. The model predicts that moderate additions of Ti, Ni, and Co increase the likelihood of room-temperature ductility, whereas high Cr contents promote brittle behavior. These predictions agree with published experimental observations, including Ti-assisted ductility through solid-solution and electronic effects and embrittlement in Cr-rich refractory alloys. Agreement between DFT-derived elastic descriptors and SVC decision-function margins further supports the physical relevance of the learned classification boundary. Although the available mechanical dataset remains limited and heterogeneous, the model captures experimentally consistent trends and provides an interpretable, physics-informed framework for screening tungsten-based RHEAs for targeted simulation and experimental validation in fusion environments.

infographic.key_insight: A physics-informed machine-learning model shows that moderate Ti, Ni, and Co additions can improve room-temperature ductility in tungsten-based refractory high-entropy alloys, while high Cr content tends to make them brittle.
infographic.engineering_relevance: The resulting design rules can help screen tungsten alloys for fusion-relevant components by prioritizing compositions with better ductility before costly experiments.
method: Curated sparse experimental dataset of tungsten-containing alloys
Built composition-based, physics-informed descriptors and compared three classifiers with nested cross-validation
Used SHAP and synthetic compositions on pseudo-ternary diagrams to interpret and map ductility trends
infographic.evidence_signal: A support vector classifier gave the best generalization in nested cross-validation, and its predictions aligned with reported experimental trends such as Ti-assisted ductility and Cr-driven embrittlement.
infographic.watch_item: The dataset is small and heterogeneous, so the learned boundaries should be treated as screening guidance rather than definitive alloy-performance rules.
infographic.paper_title: Ductility Design Rules for Tungsten based Refractory High Entropy Alloys from Sparse Experimental Datasets

title: A Combined Tight Binding with Machine Learning Potential Model for Magnesium Compounds
authors: Jiwen Yu, Arash A. Mostofi, Andrew Horsfield
abstract: We present a model for magnesium-based systems that combines density functional tight binding (DFTB) with MACE, a machine learning interatomic potential (DFTB+MACE). In this model, the conventional repulsive potential, pair potential, is replaced by a many-body MACE potential. The MACE component of the model is trained on the difference between density functional theory (DFT) energies and forces and the corresponding DFTB values, but neglecting the pair potential contribution. Using this model we performed structural relaxation of MgO-CO2 adsorption systems, molecular dynamics calculations of water clusters and phonon spectrum calculations of stable fcc-MgO and metastable bcc-MgO structures. We compare the performance of our model with a pure MACE model and with DFT. We demonstrate that the DFTB+MACE model achieves improved accuracy relative to DFTB with a pair potential, in many cases with only a moderate increase in computational cost. In addition, it can provide electronic structures that most of the machine learning potentials cannot. The training dataset, originally developed for MACE, may not fully represent all regions of the potential surface we may encounter during simulations. Expanding the dataset for a wider potential surface is expected to further enhance predictive accuracy of DFTB+MACE model. Overall, the resulting DFTB+MACE framework enables simulations at length and time scales beyond the reach of first-principles methods while retaining an explicit description of electronic structures, making it particularly attractive for studying charge-transfer in materials.

infographic.key_insight: The paper replaces DFTB’s traditional pairwise repulsive term with a many-body MACE model, creating a hybrid DFTB+MACE potential that improves accuracy while keeping electronic-structure access.
infographic.engineering_relevance: This hybrid approach enables larger-scale, longer-timescale simulations of magnesium compounds with better fidelity than standard DFTB and more interpretability than pure machine-learning potentials.
method: Train MACE on the DFT–DFTB energy/force residuals, excluding the pair-potential contribution.
Embed MACE as the replacement for the conventional DFTB repulsive potential in a combined DFTB+MACE framework.
Validate on MgO-CO2 adsorption, water-cluster molecular dynamics, and phonon spectra for fcc- and bcc-MgO.
infographic.evidence_signal: The abstract reports improved agreement with DFT compared with DFTB using a pair potential, with only moderate extra cost, across structural relaxation, MD, and phonon calculations.
infographic.watch_item: Performance may be limited where the original MACE training set does not cover the explored potential-energy surface, so broader data coverage may be needed.
infographic.paper_title: A Combined Tight Binding with Machine Learning Potential Model for Magnesium Compounds

title: Barocaloric phase transformation from data efficient fine-tuning of machine learned interatomic potentials
authors: Ludwig Hedin, Johan Klarbring
abstract: Solid-state cooling based on the barocaloric (BC) effect has emerged as promising environmentally friendly prospective alternative to conventional vapor-compression refrigeration. The search for suitable BC materials relies on efficient atomistic simulations of their phase behavior. Machine-learned interatomic potentials (MLIPs) enable such simulations at near density functional theory (DFT) accuracy, but generating the required DFT training data remains computationally demanding, which motivates development of strategies that reduce the amount of data needed to train accurate models. In this work, we use a prototypical BC material, ammonium sulfate, as a model system and investigate how small a training set can be while still reproducing the temperature-driven structural phase transformation that underlies its BC response. We train a series of MLIPs based on the MACE architecture using three strategies: training from scratch, and naive- and multihead replay fine-tuning of the MACE-MPA-0 foundation model. These strategies are evaluated on their ability to reproduce the phase transformation of ammonium sulfate in molecular dynamics simulations across a range of training-set sizes. We find that, while the MACE-MPA-0 foundation model itself fails to reproduce the transformation, and models trained from scratch break down for small datasets, fine-tuned models reproduce the transformation using as few as 5 to 10 60-atom DFT configurations. Both fine-tuning protocols yield similarly accurate results for ammonium sulfate, but we also find some indications that multihead replay fine-tuning is more robust on configurations outside the fine-tuning domain. Exploiting this data efficiency, we further show that models can be trained on small datasets and the hybrid-DFT level, and that some form of inclusion of dispersion correction is necessary to describe the phase behavior correctly.

infographic.key_insight: Data-efficient fine-tuning of a foundation ML interatomic potential can reproduce ammonium sulfate’s barocaloric phase transformation with only 5–10 DFT configurations.
infographic.engineering_relevance: This lowers the DFT data burden for screening and simulating solid-state cooling materials, making atomistic discovery of barocaloric candidates more practical.
method: Train MACE-based MLIPs from scratch or fine-tune the MACE-MPA-0 foundation model
Compare naive replay vs multihead replay fine-tuning across multiple training-set sizes
Test each model in molecular dynamics by checking whether the temperature-driven structural phase transition is recovered
infographic.evidence_signal: Across training-set sizes, fine-tuned models reproduced the phase transformation, while the foundation model alone and many from-scratch models failed; the study also reports that hybrid-DFT training and dispersion correction are needed for correct phase behavior.
infographic.watch_item: The result is demonstrated on a prototypical single material system, so transferability to other barocaloric compounds and other phase-change chemistries still needs validation.
infographic.paper_title: Barocaloric phase transformation from data efficient fine-tuning of machine learned interatomic potentials

title: Mapping the Growth of Two-Dimensional $π$-Conjugated Polymers on Au(111): Organometallic Intermediates and Edge Terminations
authors: Simon W. Briesenick, Wyatt A. Behn, Manuel González Lastre, Chang Wan Kang, Pablo Pou, Ekaterina D. Ulyanov
abstract: Kagome lattices provide an exciting space for the exploration of graphene-like $π$-conjugated molecular systems with flat bands. Using heterotriangulene-derived precursors, along with an on-surface Ullmann coupling process, makes growing polymers with Kagome lattices accessible and straightforward. Here, we use scanning tunneling microscopy alongside high-resolution atomic force microscopy to examine the evolution of tribromotrioxaazatriangulene on Au(111) into ordered, covalent films. Using density functional theory and scanning probe methods, we find previously unreported organometallic intermediate states involving Au adatoms incorporated within the growing polymer lattice. We also find that a majority of polymer edges remain brominated up to 250 $^{\circ}$C and a large number of edges bonded to Au adatoms coordinated to an adjacent bromine atom. These observations suggest that residual bromine could play a role in stabilizing the polymer edges to Au adatoms and thereby influence the growth pathways that lead to ordered Kagome polymer lattices.

infographic.key_insight: On Au(111), tribromotrioxaazatriangulene does not go directly to a covalent Kagome polymer; it forms Au-adatom–stabilized organometallic intermediates and retains brominated edge terminations up to high temperature.
infographic.engineering_relevance: Understanding these intermediate states and edge terminations helps control on-surface synthesis routes for defect- and topology-sensitive 2D pi-conjugated polymers with desired Kagome-lattice properties.
method: Scanning tunneling microscopy to track molecular ordering and growth on Au(111)
High-resolution atomic force microscopy to resolve bond-level structures and edge terminations
Density functional theory to interpret Au-adatom coordination and reaction pathways
infographic.evidence_signal: The abstract reports microscopy plus DFT evidence for previously unreported organometallic intermediates, with many polymer edges remaining brominated and/or Au-adatom coordinated up to 250 �b0C.
infographic.watch_item: Edge chemistry appears highly substrate- and temperature-dependent, so growth protocols may need tight thermal control to avoid unintended bromine retention or Au-adatom trapping.
infographic.paper_title: Mapping the Growth of Two-Dimensional $π$-Conjugated Polymers on Au(111): Organometallic Intermediates and Edge Terminations

title: A Hybrid Quantum Mechanics Machine Learning Forcefield (QM/ML) Framework for Accurate Solute-Dislocation Interaction Simulations
authors: Junting Zhang, Colleen Reynolds, Ryan S. Stroud, Jana Smutna, Daniel J. M. King, Andrew P. Horsfield
abstract: Solute-dislocation interactions play a central role in controlling microstructural evolution and mechanical behaviour of structural materials, yet conventional atomistic modelling approaches struggle to combine the chemical accuracy with computational scalability. In the nuclear industry, these challenges become particularly acute, as experiments reveal strong correlations between solute segregation and irradiation-induced dislocation loops. However, theoretical insight remains limited because density functional theory (DFT) simulations are prohibitively expensive at relevant length scales, while traditional semi-empirical interatomic potentials lack the chemical fidelity required for predictive solute-defect calculations. Here, we introduce a hybrid quantum-mechanics/machine-learning (QM/ML) simulation framework that couples DFT with neural-network machine learning interatomic potentials (MLIPs), enabling accurate atomistic dislocation simulations at reduced computational cost. We demonstrate the QM/ML framework's capability by reproducing the experimentally observed Sn and Fe segregation to dislocation loops in Zr and investigating magnetically complex solute-dislocation interactions in steel. These results establish the approach as a transferable, high-fidelity tool for modelling irradiation-induced defect structures and benchmarking emerging MLIPs.

infographic.key_insight: A hybrid DFT + neural-network ML potential framework can deliver chemically accurate solute-dislocation interaction simulations at a fraction of the cost of full quantum mechanics.
infographic.engineering_relevance: This enables more predictive modeling of irradiation-driven defect evolution and solute segregation in structural materials relevant to nuclear and other high-performance alloys.
method: Couple density functional theory with machine-learning interatomic potentials in a QM/ML workflow.
Use the hybrid model to simulate dislocation-loop interactions and solute segregation at larger atomistic scales.
Benchmark against experimentally observed Sn and Fe segregation in Zr and test magnetically complex interactions in steel.
infographic.evidence_signal: The abstract reports that the framework reproduces experimentally observed Sn and Fe segregation to dislocation loops in Zr and is also applied to steel solute-dislocation cases.
infographic.watch_item: Performance depends on the quality and transferability of the trained ML potential, especially for magnetically complex or compositionally diverse environments.
infographic.paper_title: A Hybrid Quantum Mechanics Machine Learning Forcefield (QM/ML) Framework for Accurate Solute-Dislocation Interaction Simulations

title: LocalNav: Distilling Frontier VLMs and Embodied RL for On-Device Object Goal Navigation
authors: Nicolas Baumann, Liam Boyle, Pu Deng, Edoardo Ghignone, Boyang Sun, Marc Pollefeys
abstract: Vision Language Models (VLMs) have emerged in the robotic domain as a powerful tool that enables environmental perception with language context, serving as a catalyst for open-vocabulary tasks like ObjectNav. Yet, their computational footprint typically confines them to cloud execution, hindering low-latency inference with local deployment on resource-constrained robots. To address this challenge, we present a distillation strategy that transfers complex spatial-semantic reasoning from large frontier models into a lightweight, 4B-parameter local VLM for edge execution on embedded GPU devices (e.g., Jetson Orin). We first establish a State of the Art (SotA), Scene Graph (SG)-based pipeline using Claude Sonnet 4.6, achieving a 39.7% Success Rate (SR) on the HM3D OVON benchmark. We then demonstrate that fine-tuning Qwen3.5-4B on just 500 frontier reasoning traces effectively enables navigation capabilities, yielding a SR of 34.5%, narrowing the gap to the performance of large cloud models. Finally, we introduce E-RLVR with Token Generation (TG) regularization to compress output sequence lengths for physical deployment while grounding the agent in its task. This downstream optimization reduces TG overhead by 72.1% and latency by 71.8%. Combined with quantization, this joint strategy yields a cumulative 82.8% reduction in overall inference latency without significantly sacrificing performance, presenting a viable paradigm for local, low-latency VLM execution on mobile robots.

infographic.key_insight: A small 4B on-device VLM can be distilled from frontier cloud models to perform object-goal navigation with near-state-of-the-art accuracy while dramatically reducing inference cost.
infographic.engineering_relevance: The work shows a practical path to low-latency embodied AI on embedded GPU robots, making open-vocabulary navigation more deployable without relying on cloud inference.
method: Use Claude Sonnet 4.6 to generate scene-graph-based frontier reasoning traces for navigation.
Fine-tune Qwen3.5-4B on 500 distilled traces to transfer spatial-semantic reasoning.
Apply E-RLVR with token-generation regularization plus quantization to cut sequence length and latency for edge deployment.
infographic.evidence_signal: On HM3D OVON, the frontier SG pipeline reached 39.7% success rate, distilled Qwen3.5-4B reached 34.5%, and the final optimization reduced token-generation overhead by 72.1% and latency by 71.8% (82.8% cumulative latency reduction with quantization).
infographic.watch_item: The reported gains depend on benchmark-specific distillation traces and hardware-aware optimization, so robustness across environments and real robot setups still needs broader validation.
infographic.paper_title: LocalNav: Distilling Frontier VLMs and Embodied RL for On-Device Object Goal Navigation

title: A BRAVE Alloy Design Campaign (Bayesian Risk-aware Alloy discoVery and Exploration)
authors: Mrinalini Mulukutla, Danial Khatamsaz, Trevor Hastings, Wenle Xu, Daniel Salas, Joydeep Kundu
abstract: In constrained alloy optimization, the compositions with the highest performance potential often reside at the boundary of phase stability -- where the risk of experimental failure is also highest. This work demonstrates this principle through a risk-aware Bayesian optimization campaign on single-phase FCC high-entropy alloys in the Al-V-Cr-Mn-Fe-Co-Ni-Cu system. A learned feasibility classifier, integrated directly into the multi-objective acquisition function, probabilistically penalizes candidates likely to produce failed experiments while preserving access to high-performing boundary compositions. From approximately 27,000 CALPHAD-screened candidates, 48 alloys were synthesized over three closed-loop iterations targeting five objectives (yield strength, UTS/YS ratio, strain at UTS, dynamic-to-quasi-static hardness ratio, and simulated depth of penetration), exploring 0.12\% of the feasible space. Two compositional regimes emerged: a V-rich, Ni-rich high-strength regime (UTS up to ${\sim}1480$~MPa at 50% elongation) and a Mn-containing high-ductility regime (UTS/YS up to 4.20 at $>$50% elongation). Among feasible alloys, vanadium simultaneously drives yield strength ($r = 0.84$) and sigma-phase formation ($r = 0.54$ with infeasibility); at V = 24~at.%, the three strongest alloys and three sigma failures share the same compositional point. Additionally, the strongest performing alloys cluster in a narrow region of compositional space (V $\geq$ 20 at.%, Ni $\geq$ 36 at.%), representing ${\sim}100$ of $27,074$ feasible candidates -- a probability of $P \approx 6.5 \times 10^{-6}$ under random sampling. This dual role -- consistent with the KKT prediction that constrained optima lie on active constraint boundaries -- required feasibility-aware acquisition to access; hard filtering would have excluded this region entirely.

infographic.key_insight: The best-performing alloys sit right on the edge of phase stability, so the paper’s main message is that risk-aware Bayesian optimization can find high-strength compositions that a hard-feasibility filter would miss.
infographic.engineering_relevance: For alloy design, this shows how to simultaneously optimize performance and experimental feasibility, enabling faster discovery of candidate compositions with practical synthesis success.
method: Built a Bayesian optimization loop with a learned feasibility classifier embedded in the acquisition function.
Screened ~27,000 CALPHAD candidates and synthesized 48 alloys over 3 closed-loop iterations.
Optimized five targets at once: strength, ductility, hardness-rate sensitivity, and simulated penetration performance.
infographic.evidence_signal: The campaign explored only 0.12% of the feasible space yet found alloys up to ~1480 MPa UTS at 50% elongation and identified a narrow high-performing region around V ≥ 20 at.% and Ni ≥ 36 at.%.
infographic.watch_item: The approach depends on the quality of the CALPHAD screening and feasibility classifier, so model bias or misclassification could still exclude promising chemistries or admit failed experiments.
infographic.paper_title: A BRAVE Alloy Design Campaign (Bayesian Risk-aware Alloy discoVery and Exploration)

title: Uniaxial compression of crystalline HCP titanium: an atomistic modelling study of size effects
authors: Fatemeh Safari, Konstantinos Konstantinou
abstract: Understanding the deformation behaviour of titanium is important not only for technological advances associated with industrially-relevant applications, but also essential to achieve a fundamental understanding of the mechanical properties of the relevant alloys. In this computational study, molecular-dynamics simulations are employed to ascertain the impact of model size on the mechanical response of alpha-titanium under compression. The deformation behaviour of the crystalline models is investigated as a function of different system sizes (varied by four orders of magnitude, up to 32 million atoms), and strain rates (down to 10^8 s^-1). The results show that the elastic properties remain independent of both system size and strain rate, whereas marked size effects emerge during plastic deformation. Increasing the system size of the titanium model reduces stress fluctuations, results in more homogeneous structural evolution, and stabilizes dislocation activity. Decreasing the applied strain rate requires correspondingly a larger system size to achieve equilibration and to ensure a stable behaviour for the simulated structure. The modelling results demonstrate that system size and strain rate are strongly coupled, and their combined effect governs the simulated deformation behaviour of the compressed crystalline material.

infographic.key_insight: In crystalline HCP titanium under uniaxial compression, elastic response is size- and strain-rate-independent, but plastic deformation strongly depends on model size and strain rate through dislocation-mediated instability.
infographic.engineering_relevance: For atomistic compression simulations of titanium, choosing a sufficiently large cell is essential to obtain stable, realistic plasticity and to avoid misleading stress fluctuations at low strain rates.
method: Molecular-dynamics simulations of alpha-titanium compression
System size varied across four orders of magnitude, up to 32 million atoms
Strain rates explored down to 10^8 s^-1
infographic.evidence_signal: The abstract reports systematic comparison across many system sizes and strain rates, showing unchanged elastic properties but pronounced size effects during plastic deformation.
infographic.watch_item: Results are from high-rate MD on crystalline models, so direct transfer to lower-rate or defect-rich experimental conditions may require caution.
infographic.paper_title: Uniaxial compression of crystalline HCP titanium: an atomistic modelling study of size effects

title: Entropy engineering of BF-BT-based high-entropy ceramics for ultra-high energy storage performance
authors: Yitao Jiao, Zhenhao Fan, Dawei Wang, Hai-Feng Li
abstract: Dielectric capacitors are promising for pulsed power applications, but the energy storage performance of lead-free bulk ceramics is often limited by low breakdown strength and large ferroelectric hysteresis. Herein, a high entropy perovskite oxide BaTi0.2Zr0.2Sn0.2Hf0.2Nb0.1Sc0.1O3 was introduced into the BF-BT matrix to develop lead free high entropy ferroelectric ceramics. Multicomponent B-site substitution induces lattice distortion, enhanced pseudocubic characteristics, relaxor behavior, and grain refinement. These effects suppress polarization hysteresis and electrical conduction, resulting in a significant increase in breakdown strength. A maximum breakdown strength of 840 kV/cm1 and a recoverable energy density of 10.55 J/cm3 were achieved. Entropy-induced microstructural heterogeneity promotes a more uniform electric-field distribution and delays dielectric breakdown. This work demonstrates entropy engineering as an effective route to achieving high breakdown strength and superior energy-storage performance in lead-free ferroelectric ceramics.

infographic.key_insight: Entropy engineering via multicomponent B-site substitution in BF-BT creates a more pseudocubic, relaxed ferroelectric ceramic with lower hysteresis and much higher breakdown strength.
infographic.engineering_relevance: This provides a lead-free route to high-performance dielectric capacitors for pulsed power applications with improved recoverable energy density.
method: Introduce high-entropy perovskite BaTi0.2Zr0.2Sn0.2Hf0.2Nb0.1Sc0.1O3 into the BF-BT matrix
Use multicomponent B-site substitution to induce lattice distortion, grain refinement, and relaxor behavior
Suppress polarization hysteresis and conduction while promoting more uniform electric-field distribution
infographic.evidence_signal: The abstract reports a maximum breakdown strength of 840 kV/cm and a recoverable energy density of 10.55 J/cm3.
infographic.watch_item: Performance gains may depend on precise composition control and microstructural uniformity, which can be challenging to reproduce at scale.
infographic.paper_title: Entropy engineering of BF-BT-based high-entropy ceramics for ultra-high energy storage performance

title: INCARBench: A Benchmark for Scientific Configuration in VASP INCAR by Large Language Models
authors: Bin Shao, Jixiang Li, Xinyue Zhang, Baishun Yang, Zhiyang Liu, Weichao Wang
abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly being integrated into first-principles computational workflows, yet their ability to configure scientific calculations remains poorly understood. Here, we introduce INCARBench, a benchmark for evaluating LLMs on input configuration for the Vienna Ab initio Simulation Package (VASP) through both configuration generation and repair tasks. Evaluating 19 model configurations reveals substantial capability differences among current frontier models. While several models achieve high semantic and policy accuracy, task-critical correctness remains substantially lower, demonstrating that parameter-level correctness does not necessarily imply scientifically valid configurations. Failure analysis shows that errors concentrate in physically coupled settings involving DFT+$U$, magnetism, and correlated materials, where multiple constraints must be satisfied simultaneously. Repair evaluation further reveals that correcting incorrect settings and preserving already-valid configurations are distinct capabilities, with configuration preservation remaining a major challenge. These findings establish scientific configuration as a measurable capability of large language models and provide a foundation for developing more reliable AI systems for computational materials science.

infographic.key_insight: INCARBench shows that LLMs can often propose plausible VASP INCAR settings, but scientifically valid configuration still fails when coupled physics constraints must be satisfied together.
infographic.engineering_relevance: For computational materials workflows, this means LLMs may assist with setup, but human or rule-based verification remains essential before running expensive first-principles jobs.
method: Built INCARBench to test LLMs on VASP input configuration.
Evaluated two tasks: configuration generation and configuration repair.
Assessed both semantic/policy accuracy and task-critical correctness.
infographic.evidence_signal: The study evaluated 19 model configurations and found large gaps between high semantic accuracy and lower task-critical correctness, especially for DFT+U, magnetism, and correlated-material settings.
infographic.watch_item: A model that looks correct at the parameter level may still violate physical constraints or fail to preserve already-valid settings during repair.
infographic.paper_title: INCARBench: A Benchmark for Scientific Configuration in VASP INCAR by Large Language Models

title: Tuning the optoelectronic properties of wide bandgap perovskites: Data-driven insights from combinatorial synthesis and high-throughput experimentation
authors: Alexander Wieczorek, Sergey Tsarev, Nathan Rodkey, Oleksandr Pshyk, Stefanie Frick, Maksym V. Kovalenko
abstract: The discovery and optimization of wide-bandgap lead halide perovskites (LHPs) is hindered by solution-based workflows with limited scalability. Large compositional parameter spaces present an additional challenge for materials optimization. Here, we establish an integrated, combinatorial workflow based on sequential physical vapor deposition that enables independent tuning of cation (Cs/Pb) and anion (Br/Cl) compositions. Applying automated structural, compositional, and optical characterizations across >500 samples regions of interest are rapidly screened in the quaternary Cs-Pb-Br-Cl space. From the screening, we establish a practical Cs/Pb window of 1.05-1.20 for wide bandgap perovskites, within which elevated PL yields were observed. Through in-depth analysis of the data set, we uncover a high-energy optical transition as a robust determinant for high PL yields. By combining mechanistic insight into the compositional origins of high PL efficiency with a fully integrated, high-throughput screening framework, and by openly releasing the complete multi-modal dataset, this work provides a broadly accessible benchmark to accelerate data-driven discovery of wide-bandgap perovskites.

infographic.key_insight: A combinatorial, high-throughput vapor-deposition workflow enables independent tuning of Cs/Pb and Br/Cl composition to identify wide-bandgap perovskites with high photoluminescence (PL) yields.
infographic.engineering_relevance: The study provides a scalable screening strategy and a compositional design rule (Cs/Pb = 1.05–1.20) that can accelerate optimization of lead halide perovskites for optoelectronic devices.
method: Sequential physical vapor deposition for combinatorial synthesis
Automated structural, compositional, and optical characterization
Data-driven screening across the quaternary Cs-Pb-Br-Cl space
infographic.evidence_signal: The authors screened more than 500 sample regions and found that a high-energy optical transition is a robust predictor of elevated PL yield, with best performance in a narrow Cs/Pb window.
infographic.watch_item: The design rule is derived from a specific material system and high-throughput platform, so transferability to other perovskite compositions or device stacks should be validated experimentally.
infographic.paper_title: Tuning the optoelectronic properties of wide bandgap perovskites: Data-driven insights from combinatorial synthesis and high-throughput experimentation

title: PtyRANNOSAUR: Ptychography with Robust Artificial Neural Networks Optimized for Sub-Angstrom Accuracy and Ultrafast Reconstruction
authors: Kieran Loehr, Rahim Raja, Xiaochuan Ding, Jeffrey Huang, Gillian Nolan, Sang hyun Bae
abstract: We present PtyRANNOSAUR, a data-driven neural network code that reconstructs atomic resolution electron ptychography data in seconds, 10-100x faster than standard methods. PtyRANNOSAUR uses convolutional autoencoders to map 4D-scanning transmission electron microscopy data to 2D phase images. Each model is trained on a large database of crystal structures and is tailored for a range of experimental parameters, such as accelerating voltage, convergence angle, defocus, and sample thickness. This approach yields high quality reconstructions without requiring any fine-tuning of hyperparameters. In addition, the code handles spatial partial coherence, multiple scattering, and scan position errors, which are critical for state-of-the-art electron ptychography reconstructions. By testing PtyRANNOSAUR on experimental and simulated data, we show that the neural networks accurately reconstruct atomic structures of a broad range of materials systems and can achieve high resolutions of $<0.5$ Å, comparable to the best iterative reconstructions of the same data. These advances enable near-live, state-of-the-art electron ptychography reconstructions.

infographic.key_insight: PtyRANNOSAUR uses convolutional autoencoders to turn 4D-STEM ptychography data into atomic-resolution phase images in seconds, enabling near-live reconstruction with sub-angstrom accuracy.
infographic.engineering_relevance: The method can dramatically accelerate electron ptychography workflows for materials characterization while maintaining reconstruction quality comparable to the best iterative solvers.
method: Trains CNN autoencoders on a large database of crystal structures.
Tailors separate models to experimental conditions such as voltage, convergence angle, defocus, and thickness.
Accounts for partial coherence, multiple scattering, and scan position errors.
infographic.evidence_signal: The abstract reports testing on both experimental and simulated data, with reconstructions achieving resolutions below 0.5 Å and speeds 10–100× faster than standard methods.
infographic.watch_item: Performance depends on having models trained for the relevant experimental regime, so out-of-distribution samples or conditions may still require careful validation.
infographic.paper_title: PtyRANNOSAUR: Ptychography with Robust Artificial Neural Networks Optimized for Sub-Angstrom Accuracy and Ultrafast Reconstruction

title: Lattice Reconstruction and Orbital Hybridization Suppress Magnetism in TaCo$_2$Te$_2$
authors: Ulysse Chazarin, Bharat C. Bathu, Zuned Ahmed, Marta Zonno, Chiara Bigi, Francois Bertran
abstract: Structural reconstruction in low-dimensional quantum materials can strongly modify electronic symmetry and magnetic stability through orbital hybridization. Here, we investigate the interplay between lattice reconstruction, electronic structure, and magnetic instability in the layered van der Waals compound TaCo$_2$Te$_2$ using scanning tunneling microscopy and spectroscopy (STM/STS), non-contact atomic force microscopy (nc-AFM), angle-resolved photoemission spectroscopy (ARPES), and density functional theory (DFT). While nc-AFM resolves a distorted hexagonal Te surface lattice, STM/STS reveal a pronounced square-like electronic symmetry that does not directly follow the atomic structure. ARPES further shows a strongly anisotropic Fermi surface and reconstructed low-energy states. Spatially resolved spectroscopy and orbital-projected DFT demonstrate that the bias-dependent STM contrast does not arise from a simple reversal between occupied and unoccupied states, but from the energy-integrated local density of states dominated by electronic states exhibiting opposite spatial contrast at selected energies. DFT calculations further show that reconstruction suppresses the magnetic instability present in the undistorted structure, stabilizing a nonmagnetic ground state through enhanced orbital hybridization. These results establish TaCo$_2$Te$_2$ as a model system in which lattice reconstruction reorganizes electronic symmetry and suppresses magnetism, highlighting structural reconstruction as a route for controlling correlated and magnetic phases in low-dimensional quantum materials.

infographic.key_insight: Lattice reconstruction in TaCo2Te2 reshapes orbital hybridization, producing an electronic symmetry that differs from the atomic lattice and suppressing the material’s magnetic instability.
infographic.engineering_relevance: This suggests structural reconstruction can be used as a control knob to tune electronic anisotropy and switch correlated or magnetic behavior in layered quantum materials.
method: STM/STS to map surface topology and bias-dependent electronic contrast
nc-AFM to resolve the distorted Te lattice
ARPES plus orbital-projected DFT to connect Fermi-surface reconstruction with magnetism suppression
infographic.evidence_signal: The abstract reports complementary microscopy, spectroscopy, and DFT showing a square-like electronic symmetry, anisotropic Fermi surface, and a reconstructed nonmagnetic ground state.
infographic.watch_item: The STM contrast reflects energy-integrated local density of states, so it should not be interpreted as a direct one-to-one map of the atomic positions or a simple occupied/unoccupied-state reversal.
infographic.paper_title: Lattice Reconstruction and Orbital Hybridization Suppress Magnetism in TaCo$_2$Te$_2$

title: In situ synchrotron X-ray diffraction study of flash austenitization and process design insights in medium-Manganese steels for energy applications
authors: Bowen Zou, Mathias Zapf, Thea Kannenberg, Daniel Schneider, Yixu Wang, Xiao Shen
abstract: Medium Mn steels (MMnSs) are promising candidates for energy-related infrastructure because their multiphase microstructures and austenite stability can be tailored to improve failure resistance under demanding service conditions. Flash austenitization (FA) provides a rapid route to form austenite while limiting prior austenite grain coarsening and substitutional solute homogenization, but the related short-time transformation kinetics remain insufficiently quantified. In the present work, the effects of FA temperature and initial microstructure on austenitization kinetics were investigated in an Fe-6Mn-1.5Si-1Cr-0.3Mo-0.05Nb-0.2C (wt.%) MMnS using dilatometry-integrated in situ synchrotron X-ray diffraction. Two initial microstructures produced by austenite reversion treatment (ART) were heated at 100 degrees C/s to 850 degrees C, 900 degrees C, or 950 degrees C and then held isothermally. Rapid heating alone is insufficient for full austenitization, even above the reference Ac3 temperature determined under slow heating. Full austenitization, defined by bcc fraction (f_alpha) <= 1 wt.%, requires short holding, decreasing from about 8 s at 850 degrees C to about 2 s at 950 degrees C. The final stage of austenitization is less sensitive to FA temperature than the early holding stage. The initial ART state mainly shifts the starting austenite fraction, whereas both states show comparable kinetic trends at higher FA temperatures.

infographic.key_insight: Flash austenitization in medium-Mn steel needs a brief isothermal hold to finish austenite formation, with required time dropping from about 8 s at 850°C to about 2 s at 950°C.
infographic.engineering_relevance: This gives a practical time–temperature window for designing rapid heat treatments that form stable austenite without excessive grain growth, relevant to energy infrastructure steels.
method: Dilatometry-integrated in situ synchrotron X-ray diffraction
Compared two ART-derived initial microstructures
Heated at 100°C/s to 850°C, 900°C, or 950°C, then held isothermally
infographic.evidence_signal: The abstract reports quantitative phase-fraction kinetics, showing full austenitization only when the bcc fraction falls to fα ≤ 1 wt.% after a short hold that becomes shorter at higher FA temperature.
infographic.watch_item: Results are specific to one alloy composition and the tested heating/holding schedule, so transfer to other MMn steels or larger-scale processing may require re-calibration.
infographic.paper_title: In situ synchrotron X-ray diffraction study of flash austenitization and process design insights in medium-Manganese steels for energy applications

title: Amorphous Fe-Sn nanofilms for anomalous-Nernst heat-flux sensing
authors: Kenji Tanabe, Ko Mibu, Atsushi Tsukazaki, Kohei Fujiwara
abstract: Amorphous magnetic films are promising for anomalous-Nernst heat-flux sensing because their low thermal conductivity can enhance the temperature gradient generated by an applied heat flux. However, amorphization often degrades electronic transport and thermoelectric properties, making it challenging to obtain a large anomalous Nernst response in structurally disordered films. Here, we demonstrate nanometer-thick amorphous Fe-Sn films as high-sensitivity anomalous-Nernst heat-flux sensing materials. By systematically controlling composition and thickness, we find that amorphous Fe-Sn nanofilms combine a large anomalous Nernst response with low thermal conductivity, resulting in a heat-flux sensitivity of 0.37 um/A. This value exceeds the sensitivities reported for both amorphous magnetic thin films and representative crystalline topological magnets. X-ray diffraction and Mossbauer spectroscopy show that the optimized films lack long-range crystallinity while retaining local Fe-Sn environments, suggesting that short-range atomic order contributes to the anomalous Nernst response in the amorphous matrix. The sensitivity is also reproduced on flexible polymer substrates, indicating compatibility with mechanically compliant device architectures. These results establish amorphous Fe-Sn nanofilms as a platform for anomalous-Nernst heat-flux sensing and provide a materials design route based on local-structure control and thermal-conductivity reduction.

infographic.key_insight: Nanometer-thick amorphous Fe-Sn films achieve unusually high anomalous-Nernst heat-flux sensitivity by combining a strong Nernst response with low thermal conductivity.
infographic.engineering_relevance: These films offer a flexible, thin-film sensing platform for heat-flux sensors with performance that surpasses many amorphous magnetic and crystalline topological materials.
method: Systematically varied Fe-Sn composition and film thickness.
Characterized structure with X-ray diffraction and Mössbauer spectroscopy.
Evaluated anomalous-Nernst heat-flux sensitivity and substrate flexibility.
infographic.evidence_signal: The optimized amorphous Fe-Sn nanofilms reached a heat-flux sensitivity of 0.37 μm/A and this performance was reproduced on polymer substrates.
infographic.watch_item: Device performance depends on maintaining the optimized composition/thickness and local short-range order, which may be sensitive to fabrication conditions.
infographic.paper_title: Amorphous Fe-Sn nanofilms for anomalous-Nernst heat-flux sensing

title: Thermal Corrections and Analysis on the Phase Stability of CsPbCl3 and Cs2AgSbCl6 during In-Situ Thermal Treatment
authors: Ethan R. Cronk, Wenjun Xiang, Rachel Fister, Biswajit Ball, Feng Yan, Liping Yu
abstract: Cesium lead chloride (CsPbCl3) is a well known and principal model for inorganic perovskite halide optoelectronic research. The many available techniques including high temperature stability testing have been used to investigate the increasing interest in inorganic perovskites as primary layers in solar cell applications. Due to the nature of high temperature testing, the characterization technique, reproducibility, and the true sample temperature are vital in determining relative stability. By choosing CsPbCl3 in the investigation of the structural stability of perovskites at high temperatures, it acts as a baseline to create and verify a methodology that accurately probes sample temperature, phase transitions, and decomposition onsets. Therefore, we present a methodological approach to investigate the thermal interactions and stability of CsPbCl3 as a parent single perovskite halide based on ligand-assisted re-precipitation synthesis techniques. Where we use our approach to inform and probe thermal interactions in other cesium/chlorine compounds like Cs2AgSbCl6. By analyzing the stoichiometry and initial phases through investigations of the crystalline structure and particle morphology, we calculated temperature conversions using a control substrate and refinements to best estimate changing structure parameters. Using in-situ temperature dependent X-ray diffraction, we were able to effectively probe the phase transitions and decomposition temperatures of the investigated halide powders. Creating a process that can confirm known high temperature structural phenomena of model perovskite halides while verifying our true sample temperature. Which allowed for further testing on the thermal kinetics of on the double perovskite structure Cs2AgSbCl6 and will continue to allow us to test other perovskites and perovskite families of interest in modern high temperature perovskite halide research.

infographic.key_insight: The paper establishes CsPbCl3 as a thermal calibration and stability benchmark to more accurately identify true phase-transition and decomposition temperatures in halide perovskites, then applies the same approach to Cs2AgSbCl6.
infographic.engineering_relevance: This methodology improves the reliability of high-temperature phase-stability screening for perovskite materials used in optoelectronics and solar-cell development.
method: Ligand-assisted re-precipitation synthesis of CsPbCl3 and related cesium/chlorine compounds
In-situ temperature-dependent X-ray diffraction to track phase evolution
Use of a control substrate and refinements to estimate actual sample temperature
infographic.evidence_signal: The abstract reports that the authors used crystalline-structure and morphology analysis plus in-situ XRD to confirm known thermal behavior, determine phase transitions, and identify decomposition onsets in the studied powders.
infographic.watch_item: Temperature measurements and inferred stability windows depend on calibration quality, so errors in sample temperature estimation or heating uniformity could affect the reported transition points.
infographic.paper_title: Thermal Corrections and Analysis on the Phase Stability of CsPbCl3 and Cs2AgSbCl6 during In-Situ Thermal Treatment
